Option C is incorrect because Southern blotting is used to detect specific DNA sequences but is not a high-throughput sequencing method. Option D is incorrect because flow cytometry is used for cell counting and sorting, not DNA sequencing.

questions.lo: F-A-010. Cell division and chromosomal abnormalities
questions.question: Besides stray primordial germ cells, which other pluripotent cell population is a recognized source for the development of sacrococcygeal teratomas during gastrulation?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because epiblast cells, which give rise to all three germ layers during gastrulation, are a recognized source of pluripotent cells that can form teratomas. Option B is incorrect because hypoblast cells primarily form the endoderm of the yolk sac and are not the source of the three embryonic germ layers. Option C and D are incorrect because trophoblast cells form the fetal portion of the placenta, not the embryo proper. Option E is incorrect because extraembryonic mesoderm contributes to the chorion and umbilical cord, not the pluripotent tissues found in teratomas.

questions.lo: F-A-010. Cell division and chromosomal abnormalities
questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of mitosis that distinguishes it from meiosis?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because mitosis results in two daughter cells that are genetically identical to the parent cell, whereas meiosis produces genetically diverse cells. Option A is incorrect because reduction to a haploid number is a feature of meiosis. Option B is incorrect because crossover is unique to meiosis I. Option C is incorrect because homologous pairing (synapsis) only occurs in meiosis. Option E is incorrect because mitosis involves only one round of division, while meiosis requires two.

options.A: Reduction of chromosome number to haploid
options.D: Production of genetically identical daughter cells
options.E: Two successive rounds of cell division

questions.question: A comparative study of gametogenesis shows that while one primary spermatocyte produces four functional gametes, one primary oocyte produces only one. What is the fate of the remaining three daughter cells in the female?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because during oogenesis, the meiotic divisions result in unequal cytokinesis, producing one large mature oocyte and three small polar bodies that receive minimal cytoplasm and eventually degenerate. Option B is incorrect because follicular cells are derived from the surface epithelium of the ovary, not from the meiotic division of the oocyte. Option C is incorrect because the corpus luteum is formed from the remaining granulosa and theca cells of the follicle after ovulation. Option D is incorrect because the yolk sac wall is formed from hypoblast cells, not from germ cell divisions. Option E is incorrect because theca cells are derived from the ovarian stroma (connective tissue) surrounding the follicle.

options.D: Development into the yolk sac wall

questions.question: At what specific stage of life do female germ cells first initiate the prophase of the first meiotic division?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because oogonia differentiate into primary oocytes and enter the prophase of meiosis I prenatally, specifically starting around the third month of fetal development. Option A is incorrect because meiosis begins long before birth. Option C is incorrect because puberty marks the completion of the first meiotic division, not its initiation. Option D is incorrect because primordial germ cells undergo mitosis during migration; they do not enter meiosis until they reach the gonad and differentiate into oogonia. Option E is incorrect because menarche is the result of the first completed ovarian cycle, occurring years after meiosis has already begun.

options.A: During the first week of neonatal life
options.B: During the third to seventh month of fetal development
options.C: Immediately following the onset of puberty
options.D: During the migration of primordial germ cells to the gonad
options.E: At the time of the first menstrual cycle (menarche)

questions.question: A medical student is examining a histological slide of a mature ovary. Which layer of the follicle is described as a fibrous capsule that blends with the surrounding ovarian stroma?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the theca externa is the outer fibrous layer of the follicle that gradually merges with the ovarian connective tissue (stroma). Option A is incorrect because the theca interna is the inner, highly vascularized layer of secretory cells. Option B is incorrect because the zona pellucida is a glycoprotein layer immediately surrounding the oocyte. Option C is incorrect because the corona radiata consists of granulosa cells that stay attached to the oocyte after ovulation. Option D is incorrect because the cumulus oophorus is the mound of granulosa cells in which the oocyte is embedded.

questions.question: What is the total number of chromosomes found in a human primary oocyte during the diplotene stage?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because a primary oocyte is a diploid cell that has replicated its DNA; it contains 46 double-structured chromosomes. Option B is incorrect because 23 is the haploid number found in secondary oocytes or mature gametes. Option C is incorrect because while the DNA content is doubled (4n), the chromosome count remains 46. Option D is incorrect because 44 refers only to the autosomes, excluding the sex chromosomes (XX). Option E is incorrect as it does not represent the normal human chromosomal complement.

questions.question: During the maturation of the follicle, the follicular cells proliferate to form a stratified epithelium. What is the specific name given to these cells once they form this multi-layered covering?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because as the primordial follicle grows, the flat follicular cells become cuboidal and proliferate to form a stratified layer known as granulosa cells. Option A is incorrect because lutein cells are formed from granulosa and thecal cells only after ovulation under the influence of LH. Option B is incorrect because theca cells are derived from the surrounding ovarian stroma, not the original follicular cells. Option C is incorrect because stromal cells are the general connective tissue cells of the ovary. Option E is incorrect because mesothelial cells form the surface epithelium of the ovary, not the follicular layers.

questions.question: A 24-year-old woman is being treated for infertility. Her physician explains that her oocytes are arrested in a specific stage of meiosis by a small peptide. Which of the following is the primary source of this inhibitory substance?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because Oocyte Maturation Inhibitor (OMI) is a small peptide secreted by the follicular (granulosa) cells into the microenvironment of the oocyte to maintain meiotic arrest. Option A is incorrect because theca interna cells primarily secrete androgens that are converted to estrogens. Option B is incorrect because the pituitary gland secretes FSH and LH, which eventually overcome this inhibition. Option D is incorrect because the inhibitor is a paracrine factor produced externally to the oocyte. Option E is incorrect because the corpus luteum forms after the arrest is broken and ovulation occurs.

questions.question: During the maturation of a primary follicle, a distinct space forms between the oocyte and the surrounding granulosa cells. What is the primary content of this perivitelline space immediately after the first meiotic division?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the first meiotic division results in a secondary oocyte and a first polar body; the polar body is cast off into the perivitelline space between the zona pellucida and the oocyte membrane. Option A is incorrect because follicular fluid is found within the antrum, not the perivitelline space. Option C is incorrect because the second polar body is only formed if fertilization occurs. Option D is incorrect because hyaluronic acid is a component of the extracellular matrix but not the defining content of this space post-division. Option E is incorrect as antral fluid is synonymous with follicular fluid.

questions.question: What is the specific DNA content (C-value) of a secondary oocyte compared to a normal somatic cell in the G1 phase?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because a secondary oocyte has completed meiosis I, meaning it has 23 chromosomes (haploid number), but each chromosome still consists of two sister chromatids. Therefore, its DNA content is equivalent to a diploid somatic cell in G1 (2n). Option A is incorrect because 1n would be the DNA content of a definitive oocyte after the second meiotic division. Option C is incorrect as it is not a standard value in this process. Option D is incorrect because 4n is the DNA content of the primary oocyte after DNA replication but before the first division. Option E is incorrect as it is biologically impossible in this context.

questions.question: As a primary follicle develops, the surrounding ovarian connective tissue organizes into the theca folliculi. From which specific cell population is this thecal layer derived?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the theca folliculi (both interna and externa) is formed by the organization of the ovarian stromal cells (connective tissue) surrounding the growing follicle. Option B is incorrect because surface epithelial cells give rise to the original follicular cells, not the theca. Option C is incorrect because oogonia are the germ cells themselves. Option D is incorrect because the mesonephric duct contributes to the male reproductive system, not the ovarian follicle layers. Option E is incorrect because primordial germ cells are the precursors to the oocytes.

questions.question: A researcher is observing fertilization in vitro. He notes that the secondary oocyte only completes its second meiotic division upon the entry of a spermatozoon. What is the immediate chromosomal outcome of this completed division?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the completion of meiosis II, triggered by fertilization, results in the formation of the definitive oocyte (mature egg) and the second polar body. Option A is incorrect because the division is highly unequal in cytoplasmic distribution. Option B is incorrect because the primary oocyte is the cell that exists before the first division. Option C is incorrect because the secondary oocyte is the cell that enters the second division. Option E is incorrect because the term "zygote" refers to the state after the fusion of male and female pronuclei, which happens after the second meiotic division is finished.

options.B: A primary oocyte and a polar body
options.C: A secondary oocyte and a second polar body
options.D: A definitive oocyte and a second polar body
options.E: A zygote and two polar bodies

questions.question: In a growing follicle, the oocyte and the surrounding follicular cells maintain metabolic exchange through specialized structures. Which of the following best describes the physical interaction between these cells?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because small, fingerlike cytoplasmic processes of the follicular cells extend across the zona pellucida and interdigitate with the microvilli of the oocyte's plasma membrane, allowing for the transport of materials. Option A is incorrect because the zona pellucida is an acellular glycoprotein layer, not a cellular structure that forms desmosomes. Option C is incorrect because the cells remain distinct entities. Option D is incorrect because while diffusion occurs, the specialized interdigitations are the primary mechanism for active transport and communication. Option E is incorrect because communication is essential for oocyte maturation.

options.A: Desmosomes connect the zona pellucida to the granulosa cells
options.B: Cytoplasmic processes of follicular cells interdigitate with oocyte microvilli
options.C: The oocyte membrane fuses directly with the follicular cell membranes
options.D: Simple diffusion occurs across the thick, acellular zona pellucida
options.E: Tight junctions prevent any material from passing between the layers

questions.question: A 38-year-old woman undergoes genetic screening of her polar bodies during an IVF cycle. If the first polar body is found to have 24 chromosomes due to a nondisjunction event, what is the most likely chromosomal count of the corresponding secondary oocyte?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the first meiotic division normally separates 46 double-structured chromosomes into two sets of 23. If nondisjunction occurs and one daughter cell (the polar body) receives 24, the other daughter cell (the secondary oocyte) must receive the remaining 22. Option A is incorrect because that would imply a gain of a chromosome without a corresponding loss. Option B is incorrect as it represents a normal division. Option C is incorrect because that is the diploid number of the parent cell. Option E is incorrect as it does not follow the logic of a single pair failing to separate.

questions.question: During the transition to a vesicular follicle, the antrum begins to form. What is the primary mechanism by which this fluid-filled cavity is created?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because as development continues, fluid-filled spaces appear between the granulosa cells; the coalescence of these spaces eventually forms the single large cavity known as the antrum. Option A is incorrect because the oocyte does not secrete the antral fluid. Option B is incorrect because the zona pellucida remains intact around the oocyte. Option D is incorrect because the surface epithelium does not invaginate to form the antrum. Option E is incorrect because the theca interna remains as a peripheral layer and does not undergo central apoptosis to form the cavity.

options.A: Active secretion of water by the oocyte nucleus
options.B: Breakdown of the zona pellucida in a localized area
options.C: Coalescence of fluid-filled spaces between granulosa cells
options.D: Invagination of the surface epithelium into the follicle core
options.E: Apoptosis of the central-most theca interna cells

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the histological appearance of the nucleus in a primary oocyte during its prolonged arrest in the diplotene stage?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the diplotene stage of prophase I, where primary oocytes remain arrested until puberty or later, is characterized by a lacy network of chromatin. Option A is incorrect because heterochromatin represents inactive, tightly coiled DNA, whereas the arrested oocyte maintains a more open "lacy" structure. Option B is incorrect because while nucleoli are present, they are not the defining characteristic of the diplotene "resting" stage. Option D is incorrect because chromosomes only become highly coiled and visible as distinct structures during the active phases of mitosis or meiosis, not during the prolonged diplotene arrest. Option E is incorrect because the nuclear envelope remains intact during this resting stage to protect the genetic material.

questions.question: At which specific point in embryonic development do primordial germ cells typically reach the developing gonads?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because primordial germ cells (PGCs) migrate from the yolk sac and arrive at the primitive gonads by the end of the fifth week of development. Option A is incorrect because this is when PGCs are first formed in the epiblast. Option B is incorrect because the fourth week marks the beginning of their migration from the yolk sac wall. Option D is incorrect because by the third month, oogonia in females are already arranging in clusters. Option E is incorrect because by the seventh month, the majority of oogonia have already degenerated or become primary oocytes.

questions.question: A 26-year-old woman is undergoing an infertility workup. Her clinician explains that a specific hormonal surge is required to trigger the completion of the first meiotic division. Which of the following is the immediate result of this surge in the oocyte?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the LH surge induces the completion of Meiosis I, resulting in two daughter cells of unequal size: the large secondary oocyte and the small first polar body. Option A is incorrect because spermatids are products of male gametogenesis. Option B is incorrect because the surge causes the oocyte to *leave* the prophase I arrest. Option D is incorrect because the diplotene stage is the resting phase that begins before birth. Option E is incorrect because the second meiotic division only completes if fertilization occurs.

options.B: Arrest of the cell in prophase I
options.C: Production of a secondary oocyte and a polar body
options.E: Immediate completion of the second meiotic division

questions.question: In the kinetics of male gametogenesis, what is the approximate duration that a primary spermatocyte remains in prophase I before completing the first meiotic division?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because primary spermatocytes enter a prolonged prophase that lasts approximately 22 days, followed by a rapid completion of meiosis I. Option A is incorrect because 7 hours is the approximate time required for sperm capacitation. Option B is incorrect because 37 hours is the duration of the mature vesicular stage of a follicle before ovulation. Option D is incorrect because 74 days is the total time required for a spermatogonium to develop into a mature spermatozoon. Option E is incorrect because this refers to the potential duration of prophase I in human females.

questions.question: A researcher is studying the endocrine interactions within the ovarian follicle. They find that theca interna cells produce androstenedione, which is then converted into 17 β-estradiol by another cell type. Which cell type performs this conversion and under the influence of which hormone?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because theca interna cells produce androgens (androstenedione/testosterone), which are then converted to estrogens (estrone/estradiol) by granulosa cells, a process stimulated by Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH). Option B is incorrect because Sertoli cells are found in the testes, and LH primarily acts on Leydig cells. Option C is incorrect because lutein cells are formed after ovulation and primarily secrete progesterone. Option D and E are incorrect because hypoblast and epiblast cells are embryonic disc components, not follicular cells.

options.A: Granulosa cells under the influence of FSH
options.B: Sertoli cells under the influence of LH
options.C: Lutein cells under the influence of Progesterone
options.D: Hypoblast cells under the influence of GnRH
options.E: Epiblast cells under the influence of hCG

questions.question: During the process of spermiogenesis, a specialized lysosomal-like structure called the acrosome is formed. What is the specific anatomical extent of this structure in a mature spermatozoon?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the acrosome forms a cap that covers the anterior half of the nucleus and contains enzymes (like acrosin) to penetrate the egg. Option A is incorrect because the tail is used for motility and does not have an acrosomal covering. Option B is incorrect because the middle piece contains mitochondria. Option D is incorrect because residual bodies are the discarded cytoplasm phagocytized by Sertoli cells. Option E is incorrect because the centriole is involved in forming the neck and flagellum.

options.A: It covers the entire surface of the tail.
options.B: It is located exclusively within the middle piece.
options.C: It covers the anterior half of the nuclear surface.
options.D: It is found only within the residual bodies.
options.E: It forms a ring structure around the centriole.

questions.question: During the first meiotic division of human gametes, what is the typical number of crossovers that occur per cell?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because in humans, approximately 30 to 40 crossovers (one or two per chromosome) occur during the first meiotic division. Option A is incorrect because this is the number of crossovers per individual chromosome, not the whole cell. Option B is incorrect as it is too low for the total human genome. Option D is incorrect as it is significantly higher than the observed frequency. Option E is incorrect because the number of crossovers is not strictly tied to the number of chromosomes in a 1:1 ratio.

questions.question: When comparing the proliferative phases of male and female germ cells, which of the following statements accurately describes a fundamental difference?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the total number of germ cells in the female ovary reaches its maximum (7 million) at the fifth month of prenatal development, after which atresia begins. Option A is incorrect because females do not produce new oogonia after birth. Option B is incorrect because males begin continuous spermatogenesis only at puberty. Option D is incorrect because the diplotene arrest is a feature of oogenesis, not spermatogenesis. Option E is incorrect because females have a finite supply of oocytes established before birth.

options.A: Female germ cells continue mitotic divisions until menopause.
options.B: Male germ cells complete all mitotic divisions before birth.
options.C: Female germ cells reach their maximum number during the fifth month of fetal life.
options.D: Male germ cells are arrested in the diplotene stage from birth until puberty.
options.E: Female germ cells undergo continuous stem cell renewal throughout adult life.

questions.question: If a secondary oocyte is released during ovulation but fails to encounter a spermatozoon in the ampulla of the uterine tube, what is its most likely fate?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because if fertilization does not occur, the secondary oocyte (arrested in metaphase II) degenerates approximately 24 hours after ovulation. Option A is incorrect because the completion of meiosis II is triggered by the entry of a sperm. Option B is incorrect because the window of viability for the oocyte is much shorter than 7 days. Option D is incorrect because implantation requires a fertilized zygote that has reached the blastocyst stage. Option E is incorrect because the meiotic process is unidirectional and cannot be reversed.

options.A: It completes the second meiotic division spontaneously.
options.B: It remains viable for up to 7 days waiting for fertilization.
options.C: It undergoes degeneration approximately 24 hours after ovulation.
options.D: It implants into the tubal mucosa as a blighted ovum.
options.E: It reverts to a primary oocyte and returns to the ovary.

questions.question: In the adult male, which cells are responsible for providing physical support, protection, and nutrition to the developing germ cells within the seminiferous tubules?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because Sertoli (sustentacular) cells support and protect germ cells, participate in their nutrition, and assist in the release of mature spermatozoa. Option A is incorrect because Leydig cells are located in the interstitium and produce testosterone. Option B is incorrect because theca interna cells are found in the female ovary. Option D is incorrect because follicular cells are the female equivalent of Sertoli cells. Option E is incorrect because lutein cells are part of the corpus luteum in the ovary.

questions.question: A 32-year-old woman is found to have a condition where her primary oocytes do not remain in the resting stage of prophase I. Normally, this arrest is maintained by a substance secreted by the surrounding cells. What is this substance?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because Oocyte Maturation Inhibitor (OMI), a small peptide secreted by follicular cells, is responsible for maintaining the primary oocyte in the arrested diplotene stage of prophase I. Option A is incorrect because LH is the hormone that *breaks* the arrest. Option C is incorrect because GDF-9 is involved in the proliferation of follicular cells. Option D is incorrect because androstenedione is a precursor for estrogen. Option E is incorrect because hyaluronic acid is a component of the follicular fluid and extracellular matrix.

questions.question: During the process of spermatogenesis, cytokinesis is characteristically incomplete. What is the functional significance of the resulting cytoplasmic bridges between the developing germ cells?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because incomplete cytokinesis results in cytoplasmic bridges that join successive generations of germ cells, ensuring that the progeny of a single type A spermatogonium develop as a synchronized clone. Option A is incorrect because each cell maintains its own nucleus. Option C is incorrect because germ cells remain embedded in Sertoli cells throughout development. Option D is incorrect because the acrosome is formed during spermiogenesis, not as a result of these bridges. Option E is incorrect because all spermatids must undergo both meiotic divisions to become haploid.

options.A: They allow the cells to share a single large nucleus.
options.B: They ensure that the progeny of a single spermatogonium develop as a synchronized clone.
options.C: They prevent the cells from being recognized by Sertoli cells.
options.D: They facilitate the immediate shedding of the acrosomal cap.
options.E: They allow the sperm to bypass the second meiotic division.

questions.question: Which specific growth factor, belonging to the TGF-β family, is primarily responsible for mediating the proliferation of follicular cells during the ovarian cycle?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Growth differentiation factor 9 (GDF-9) is a member of the TGF-β family specifically identified as the mediator for the proliferation of granulosa (follicular) cells. Option B is incorrect because FGF8 is primarily involved in limb development and gastrulation. Option C is incorrect because BMP4 is involved in ventralizing mesoderm and inducing epidermis. Option D is incorrect because IGF-1 is a major growth-promoting factor for fetal tissues but not the primary mediator of follicular cell proliferation. Option E is incorrect because VEGF is the primary factor for angiogenesis and vasculogenesis.

questions.question: During the maturation of a vesicular follicle, the surrounding ovarian connective tissue organizes into two distinct layers. Which of the following best describes the theca externa?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the theca externa is the outer layer of the theca folliculi, characterized as a fibrous capsule that gradually blends into the surrounding ovarian connective tissue. Option A is incorrect because it describes the theca interna, which is secretory and vascular. Option C is incorrect because it describes the granulosa layer. Option D is incorrect because it describes the zona pellucida. Option E is incorrect because the cumulus oophorus is formed by granulosa cells, not theca cells.

options.A: An inner layer of secretory cells rich in blood vessels.
options.B: An outer fibrous capsule that merges with the ovarian stroma.
options.C: A stratified epithelium of granulosa cells.
options.D: A glycoprotein layer directly surrounding the oocyte.
options.E: A cluster of cells that forms the cumulus oophorus.

questions.question: A 26-year-old woman is monitoring her basal body temperature to identify her fertile window. She notices a slight but consistent rise in her morning temperature. This physiological change is most directly associated with the secretion of which hormone?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because progesterone, secreted by the corpus luteum after ovulation, has a thermogenic effect that causes a rise in basal body temperature. Option A is incorrect because estriol is a placental estrogen of pregnancy. Option B is incorrect because the LH surge triggers ovulation but does not directly maintain the temperature rise. Option D is incorrect because FSH is involved in follicular recruitment. Option E is incorrect because estradiol levels peak before ovulation and do not cause the post-ovulatory temperature rise.

questions.question: At the time of ovulation, the secondary oocyte is released from the mature vesicular follicle. At what specific stage is this oocyte arrested just before it is discharged from the ovary?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the oocyte completes Meiosis I just before ovulation and enters Meiosis II, where it arrests in metaphase approximately 3 hours before being discharged. Option A is incorrect because this is the stage of the primary oocyte in primordial follicles. Option B is incorrect because anaphase I is completed during the preovulatory surge. Option C is incorrect because the oocyte passes through prophase II quickly to reach the metaphase II arrest. Option E is incorrect because telophase II is only reached if fertilization occurs.

questions.question: A 32-year-old woman undergoes a laparoscopic procedure just before her expected ovulation. The surgeon observes a mature Graafian follicle. Inside this follicle, the LH surge has already triggered the completion of the first meiotic division. This is achieved by the LH surge elevating the concentration of which of the following?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the LH surge induces the completion of Meiosis I by increasing the concentrations of maturation-promoting factor (MPF). Option A is incorrect because Oocyte maturation inhibitor (OMI) keeps the oocyte arrested in prophase I; its influence must be overcome. Option B is incorrect because cAMP levels generally decrease to allow the resumption of meiosis. Option C is incorrect because GnRH is a hypothalamic hormone that stimulates the pituitary, not the oocyte directly. Option D is incorrect because hCG is produced by the syncytiotrophoblast after implantation.

questions.question: If fertilization does not occur, the corpus luteum undergoes luteolysis. What is the histological outcome of this degeneration?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the corpus albicans is the mass of fibrotic scar tissue formed when the corpus luteum shrinks and degenerates. Option B is incorrect because a corpus atreticum is formed when a follicle degenerates before reaching maturity. Option C is incorrect because this is the corpus luteum of pregnancy. Option D is incorrect because a primary follicle is an early stage of follicular development. Option E is incorrect because the stigma is the avascular spot on the ovary surface where ovulation occurs.

questions.question: During the final stages of the ovarian cycle, an avascular spot appears at the apex of the bulging vesicular follicle on the ovarian surface. What is the primary mechanism leading to the formation of this stigma?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the LH surge increases collagenase activity, which digests collagen fibers, and the resulting pressure/ischemia leads to the appearance of the avascular stigma. Option A is incorrect because theca externa is fibrous but its contraction (via prostaglandins) helps extrude the oocyte, not form the stigma. Option C is incorrect because follicular pressure actually increases or remains high until rupture. Option D is incorrect because the surface epithelium actually thins and breaks at the stigma. Option E is incorrect because lutein pigment accumulates after ovulation to form the corpus luteum.

options.A: Contraction of the theca externa fibers.
options.B: Increased collagenase activity and local ischemia.
options.C: Sudden decrease in follicular fluid pressure.

questions.question: Following the completion of the first meiotic division during the preovulatory stage, two daughter cells of unequal size are formed. What is the typical fate of the smaller cell, the first polar body?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the first polar body, which receives minimal cytoplasm, may undergo a second meiotic division but ultimately degenerates. Option A is incorrect because the larger cell (secondary oocyte) becomes the definitive oocyte. Option B is incorrect because polar bodies do not participate in fertilization. Option D is incorrect because it degenerates long before implantation. Option E is incorrect because the centrosome is typically provided by the spermatozoon.

options.A: It develops into the definitive oocyte.
options.B: It fuses with the second polar body to form the zygote.
options.C: It may undergo a second division before degenerating.
options.D: It remains in the perivitelline space until implantation.
options.E: It provides the centrosome for the first mitotic division.

questions.question: During the menstrual phase of the uterine cycle, the majority of the endometrium is shed. Which specific layer is retained to serve as the source for regenerating the glands and arteries in the subsequent proliferative phase?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the basal layer is the only part of the endometrium retained during menstruation; it has its own blood supply (basal arteries) and functions as the regenerative layer. Option A is incorrect because the compact layer is part of the functional layer that is shed. Option B is incorrect because the spongy layer is part of the functional layer that is shed. Option C is incorrect because the functional layer is the collective term for the shed compact and spongy layers. Option E is incorrect because the decidual layer refers to the endometrium during pregnancy.

questions.question: A 30-year-old woman with irregular cycles is prescribed clomiphene citrate to assist with conception. This drug works by increasing the concentration of a specific hormone to stimulate the development of multiple follicles. Which hormone is directly increased by this medication?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because clomiphene citrate is used to increase FSH concentrations, which rescues more follicles from atresia and stimulates their growth. Option A is incorrect because progesterone is a post-ovulatory hormone. Option B is incorrect because clomiphene actually acts as an anti-estrogen at the hypothalamus to trigger the rise in FSH. Option C is incorrect because while LH may rise, the primary therapeutic target for follicular recruitment is FSH. Option D is incorrect because hCG is a pregnancy hormone.

questions.question: In the coordinated production of estrogens within the maturing follicle, theca interna cells produce androgens. Which of the following describes the specific conversion that occurs within the granulosa cells?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because theca interna cells produce androstenedione and testosterone, which are then converted by granulosa cells into estrone and 17β-estradiol. Option A is incorrect because testosterone is an androgen and progesterone is a progestin; the conversion is toward estrogens. Option B is incorrect because this conversion occurs in the theca cells. Option D is incorrect because this is part of the androgenic pathway in the theca cells. Option E is incorrect because the conversion is from androgens to estrogens, not the reverse.

questions.question: During the process of ovulation, local muscular contractions occur in the ovarian wall to extrude the oocyte. Which of the following molecules increases in response to the LH surge to mediate these contractions?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because prostaglandin levels increase in response to the LH surge and cause local muscular contractions in the ovarian wall that help extrude the oocyte. Option A is incorrect because MPF is involved in nuclear maturation (meiosis), not muscle contraction. Option B is incorrect because collagenase digests the connective tissue but does not cause contraction. Option C is incorrect because hyaluronic acid is involved in the expansion of the cumulus oophorus. Option D is incorrect because OMI inhibits maturation.

questions.question: Which specific enzyme is upregulated by the LH surge to weaken the follicular wall at the stigma?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the high concentration of LH increases collagenase activity, which digests the collagen fibers surrounding the follicle, leading to its rupture. Option A is incorrect because hyaluronidase is primarily associated with sperm penetrating the cumulus oophorus during fertilization. Option C is incorrect because elastase is not the primary enzyme involved in the breakdown of the follicular wall during ovulation. Option D is incorrect because neuraminidase is an enzyme often associated with viral entry or specific cell surface modifications, not ovulation. Option E is incorrect because while trypsin-like substances are involved in the acrosome reaction, they are not the primary enzymes responsible for follicular rupture at the stigma.

questions.question: A 26-year-old woman is at day 14 of her menstrual cycle. Which hormonal change is primarily responsible for increasing the rhythmic contractions of the uterine tube to facilitate oocyte capture?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the LH surge not only triggers ovulation but also increases prostaglandin levels, which cause local muscular contractions in the ovarian wall and uterine tube. Option A is incorrect because estrogen levels are high prior to ovulation to stimulate the LH surge; a drop does not trigger these contractions. Option B is incorrect because progesterone rises significantly only after the corpus luteum forms following ovulation. Option D is incorrect because prostaglandins actually increase, not decrease, to facilitate the muscular movements required for oocyte transport. Option E is incorrect because FSH is primarily involved in the recruitment and maturation of the follicles earlier in the cycle.

questions.question: During a laparoscopic observation of the pelvic cavity mid-cycle, a surgeon notes the fimbriae of the uterine tube closely applied to the ovary. This positioning is primarily maintained by which mechanism?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because shortly before ovulation, the fimbriae of the uterine tube begin to sweep over the surface of the ovary and the tube itself begins to contract rhythmically to ensure the oocyte is captured. Option A is incorrect because the suspensory ligament contains vessels and nerves but does not actively contract to position the fimbriae. Option C is incorrect because there is no significant negative pressure from the uterus that acts to move the fimbriae. Option D is incorrect because while cilia create a current, they do not create a vacuum strong enough to move the anatomical position of the tube. Option E is incorrect because the ovarian ligament connects the ovary to the uterus and does not control the fimbriae's movement.

options.A: Contraction of the suspensory ligament of the ovary
options.B: Rhythmic muscular contractions of the tube itself
options.C: Suction created by the negative pressure of the uterus
options.D: Ciliary beating creating a vacuum in the pelvic cavity

questions.question: A researcher is studying the movement of the oocyte-cumulus complex. They observe that once the complex enters the ampulla, the cumulus cells lose contact with the oocyte. What is the specific mechanism for this loss of contact?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because once the oocyte is inside the uterine tube, the cumulus cells withdraw their cytoplasmic processes from the zona pellucida, thereby losing contact with the oocyte. Option A is incorrect because the loss of contact is a cellular retraction process, not a digestive one. Option C is incorrect because mechanical shearing is not the biological mechanism described for the separation of the cumulus-oocyte complex. Option D is incorrect because cumulus cells do not immediately undergo apoptosis; they remain as the corona radiata for a period. Option E is incorrect because the tubal epithelium facilitates transport via cilia and secretions but does not phagocytize the cumulus cells.

options.A: Digestion of the cumulus by tubal enzymes
options.B: Withdrawal of cytoplasmic processes from the zona pellucida
options.C: Mechanical shearing due to tubal peristalsis
options.D: Apoptosis of the cumulus cells upon contact with tubal fluid
options.E: Phagocytosis of the cumulus by the tubal epithelium

questions.question: A 29-year-old woman with regular cycles has sexual intercourse on the day of ovulation. If fertilization occurs, the resulting embryo typically remains in the ampullary region of the uterine tube for approximately how long before the "isthmic block" relaxes?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the fertilized oocyte (or embryo) reaches the uterine lumen approximately 3 to 4 days (72 to 96 hours) after fertilization, meaning it spends the majority of that time in the tube, specifically delayed at the isthmus. Option A is incorrect as 12 hours is too short; the embryo is still in the early cleavage stages in the ampulla. Option B is incorrect because at 24 hours, the zygote is usually just completing its first cleavage. Option C is incorrect because 48 hours is insufficient for the transition through the isthmus to the uterus. Option E is incorrect because by 120 hours (5 days), the blastocyst has typically already begun the process of implantation in the uterine wall.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the "stigma" during the process of oocyte release?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the stigma is an avascular spot that appears at the apex of the bulging vesicular follicle on the surface of the ovary, which eventually ruptures to release the oocyte. Option A is incorrect because the stigma is a structural feature of the follicle, not a chemical signal for sperm. Option B is incorrect because fimbriae sweep over the ovary but do not specifically "attach" to the stigma. Option D is incorrect because the fluid is contained within the antrum of the follicle; the stigma is the site of the wall's breakdown. Option E is incorrect because the stigma is a macroscopic area of the follicular wall, not a microscopic basement membrane.

options.A: It acts as a chemoattractant site for sperm
options.B: It serves as the attachment point for the fimbriae
options.C: It is the avascular zone where the follicular wall ruptures
options.D: It secretes the fluid that carries the oocyte into the tube
options.E: It forms the basement membrane of the granulosa cells

questions.question: In a patient with Kartagener syndrome (immotile cilia syndrome), oocyte transport is severely impaired. Which of the following is the most likely clinical consequence regarding gamete transportation?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the oocyte is carried into the tube by the sweeping movements of the fimbriae and the motion of cilia on the epithelial lining; without functional cilia, the oocyte often fails to enter the ostium. Option A is incorrect because follicular rupture is mediated by LH, collagenase, and prostaglandins, not cilia. Option B is incorrect because ciliary dysfunction leads to infertility or ectopic pregnancy, not an increase in the number of oocytes released. Option C is incorrect because the sweeping movement of the fimbriae is primarily a muscular/peristaltic action of the tube, though cilia are needed for the actual "pick-up." Option E is incorrect because lack of cilia would slow down or stop transport, not accelerate it.

options.A: Failure of the follicle to rupture at the stigma
options.C: Inability of the fimbriae to sweep the ovary
options.D: Failure of the oocyte to enter the uterine tube
options.E: Rapid transit of the zygote to the uterus

questions.question: Following ovulation, the oocyte is arrested in metaphase of meiosis II. If fertilization does not occur, what is the specific timeframe in which the oocyte typically begins to degenerate?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because if the oocyte is not fertilized, it typically degenerates approximately 24 hours after ovulation. Option A is incorrect because the oocyte remains viable and capable of being fertilized for longer than 6 hours. Option B is incorrect because 12 hours is still within the peak window for fertilization. Option D is incorrect because by 48 hours, an unfertilized oocyte has already begun significant morphological breakdown. Option E is incorrect because 72 hours is the time it takes for a *fertilized* egg to reach the uterus; an unfertilized one would have degenerated long before.

questions.question: Which of the following structures is formed by the rearrangement of cumulus oophorus cells around the zona pellucida after the oocyte is discharged?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because some of the cumulus oophorus cells rearrange themselves around the zona pellucida to form the corona radiata when the oocyte is ovulated. Option A is incorrect because the theca interna is a layer of the follicle that remains in the ovary to help form the corpus luteum. Option B is incorrect because the corpus albicans is the fibrous scar tissue formed after the corpus luteum degenerates. Option D is incorrect because the antrum is the fluid-filled cavity within the follicle that disappears upon rupture. Option E is incorrect because the stigma is the site on the ovary where rupture occurred.

questions.question: A 30-year-old woman is found to have an oocyte located in the rectouterine pouch (Pouch of Douglas). This suggests a failure in which specific step of gamete transportation?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the fimbriae are responsible for sweeping the oocyte into the uterine tube; if this "pick-up" fails, the oocyte can fall into the peritoneal cavity and settle in the most dependent part, the rectouterine pouch. Option A is incorrect because the presence of the oocyte in the pouch implies it was successfully matured and released. Option C is incorrect because isthmic transport occurs after the oocyte has already entered the tube. Option D is incorrect because luteinization occurs within the ovary to form the corpus luteum and does not affect the physical location of the released oocyte. Option E is incorrect because ampullary peristalsis moves the oocyte once it is already inside the tube.

questions.question: The rate of oocyte transport through the uterine tube is primarily regulated by the endocrine status of the patient. Which hormonal state specifically promotes the relaxation of the tubal musculature to allow the embryo to enter the uterus?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the rate of transport is regulated by endocrine status; specifically, the rising levels of progesterone (produced by the corpus luteum) after ovulation help modulate tubal motility and relax the isthmus to allow the embryo to pass into the uterine cavity. Option A is incorrect because high estrogen alone (pre-ovulatory) increases tubal activity and fimbrial vascularity but does not facilitate the final entry of the embryo into the uterus. Option B is incorrect because low estrogen and low LH are characteristic of the early follicular phase or post-menopause, not the transport phase. Option D is incorrect because the LH surge occurs at ovulation, days before the embryo reaches the uterine junction. Option E is incorrect because low progesterone would not provide the necessary signals for the "isthmic block" to relax.

questions.question: During the transport of the oocyte through the uterine tube, what is the primary role of the fluid current created by the mucosal cilia?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the oocyte is propelled through the tube by a combination of peristaltic contractions and the action of cilia on the tubal mucosa, which create a current directed toward the uterine cavity. Option A is incorrect because the zona pellucida must remain intact until the blastocyst stage. Option B is incorrect because while tubal fluid contains nutrients, the primary purpose of the *current* is transport, not nutrition. Option D is incorrect because the ciliary current does not prevent sperm migration; sperm move against the current or are aided by other mechanisms. Option E is incorrect because the second meiotic division is triggered by the entry of a spermatozoon (fertilization), not by ciliary action.

options.A: To wash away the zona pellucida
options.B: To provide nutrients to the cumulus cells
options.C: To facilitate the movement of the oocyte toward the uterus
options.D: To prevent sperm from reaching the ampulla
options.E: To trigger the second meiotic division

questions.question: During the fusion of gametes, which specific part of the spermatozoon membrane establishes the initial contact with the oocyte plasma membrane?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because during the acrosome reaction, the plasma membrane covering the acrosomal head cap disappears, leaving the membrane of the posterior region of the sperm head to fuse with the oocyte. Option A is incorrect as the inner acrosomal membrane is exposed but does not facilitate the primary fusion with the oocyte. Option B is incorrect because this part of the membrane is lost during the acrosome reaction before fusion occurs. Option D is incorrect as the middle piece contains mitochondria and does not participate in the initial membrane fusion. Option E is incorrect because the outer acrosomal membrane fuses with the overlying plasma membrane to create pores during the acrosome reaction.

options.B: The plasma membrane covering the acrosomal cap
options.C: The plasma membrane of the posterior region of the head
options.D: The membrane of the middle piece

questions.question: A 28-year-old woman is undergoing an in vitro fertilization (IVF) procedure. The embryologist observes the oocytes several hours after the introduction of capacitated sperm. The presence of which structure is the most reliable indicator that the second meiotic division has been successfully completed?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the second meiotic division is only completed upon the entry of the spermatozoon, resulting in the formation of the definitive oocyte and the second polar body. Option A is incorrect because the male pronucleus forms from the swelling of the sperm nucleus, which occurs after the division is already triggered. Option B is incorrect as the first polar body is formed during the first meiotic division at the time of ovulation. Option D is incorrect because the zona pellucida is a pre-existing extracellular matrix. Option E is incorrect because while the tail enters the cytoplasm, its presence does not define the completion of meiosis.

options.E: The sperm tail within the cytoplasm

questions.question: In the molecular orchestration of fertilization, the binding of the spermatozoon to the zona pellucida is a critical step. Which of the following events occurs as a direct immediate consequence of the sperm head interacting with the ZP3 ligand?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because ZP3 acts as a specific receptor on the zona pellucida that binds the sperm and induces the acrosome reaction, allowing the release of enzymes like acrosin. Option A is incorrect because the zona reaction is triggered by the fusion of the sperm and oocyte plasma membranes, not the initial binding to ZP3. Option B is incorrect as the resumption of meiosis II is triggered by sperm entry into the cytoplasm. Option D is incorrect because the removal of the glycoprotein coat occurs during capacitation in the uterine tube before the sperm reaches the zona. Option E is incorrect because the pronuclei lose their envelopes much later, just before the first mitotic cleavage.

options.A: Initiation of the zona reaction to prevent polyspermy
options.B: Completion of the second meiotic division of the oocyte
options.C: Induction of the acrosome reaction and release of acrosin
options.D: Removal of the glycoprotein coat from the sperm plasma membrane
options.E: Fusion of the male and female pronuclear envelopes

questions.question: What is the fate of the spermatozoon's plasma membrane after it successfully penetrates the oocyte?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because during Phase 3 of fertilization (fusion of membranes), the plasma membranes of the sperm and egg fuse, but the actual sperm membrane remains on the oocyte surface while the head and tail enter the cytoplasm. Option A is incorrect as the nuclear envelope of the zygote is formed independently during pronuclear development. Option C is incorrect because the zona pellucida is modified by lysosomal enzymes from cortical granules, not the sperm membrane. Option D is incorrect as the second polar body is a product of oocyte cytoplasmic division. Option E is incorrect because cortical granules release their contents into the perivitelline space; they do not phagocytose the sperm membrane.

options.A: It is incorporated into the nuclear envelope of the zygote.
options.B: It is left behind on the exterior surface of the oocyte.
options.C: It dissolves the zona pellucida from the inside.
options.D: It forms the primary component of the second polar body.
options.E: It is phagocytosed by the cortical granules.

questions.question: During the final stages of fertilization, the male and female pronuclei prepare for the first cleavage division. At what specific point does the replication of DNA occur in these haploid nuclei?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because each haploid pronucleus (23 chromosomes, 1n DNA) must replicate its DNA (becoming 23 chromosomes, 2n DNA) during its growth phase so that the resulting zygote has the correct amount of DNA for its first division. Option A is incorrect as the second meiotic division only produces the haploid female set; replication follows later. Option B is incorrect because the sperm is still a condensed gamete at this stage. Option D is incorrect because the pronuclei do not "fuse" into a single nucleus; they lose their envelopes and their chromosomes intermingle on a shared spindle. Option E is incorrect because DNA replication must be completed before the chromosomes organize on the spindle.

options.A: Immediately after the second meiotic division of the oocyte
options.B: During the migration of the sperm through the corona radiata
options.C: During the growth of the pronuclei before their envelopes disappear
options.D: After the two pronuclei have fused into a single diploid nucleus
options.E: Only after the first mitotic spindle has formed in the zygote

questions.question: A researcher is studying the prevention of polyspermy in human zygotes. They observe that immediately after a single sperm contacts the oocyte surface, the oocyte releases lysosomal enzymes from cortical granules. What is the primary functional outcome of this specific "zona reaction"?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the zona reaction involves the release of cortical granules that alter the structure of the zona pellucida to prevent further sperm binding and penetration by inactivating receptor sites (like ZP3). Option A is incorrect because the detachment of the sperm tail is a separate degenerative process within the cytoplasm. Option B is incorrect as metabolic activation is a different response involving the "awakening" of the egg's machinery. Option D is incorrect because zygote transport is mediated by ciliary action and peristalsis of the uterine tube. Option E is incorrect because the male pronucleus forms due to the swelling of the sperm nucleus, not the zona reaction.

options.A: It triggers the immediate detachment of the sperm tail.
options.B: It provides the metabolic energy required for cleavage.
options.C: It hardens the zona pellucida and inactivates sperm receptors.
options.D: It facilitates the transport of the zygote to the uterus.
options.E: It induces the formation of the male pronucleus.

questions.question: Which of the following terms describes the oocyte immediately after it has completed the second meiotic division but before the pronuclei have formed?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the "definitive oocyte" is the mature female gamete produced at the end of the second meiotic division. Option A is incorrect as the primary oocyte is the cell arrested in prophase I since birth. Option B is incorrect because the secondary oocyte is the cell arrested in metaphase II until fertilization. Option D is incorrect because the zygote is the diploid cell formed after the maternal and paternal chromosomes intermingle. Option E is incorrect as the morula is a 16-cell stage occurring 3 days after fertilization.

questions.question: Capacitation is a vital functional maturation process for spermatozoa. Which specific change occurs to the sperm during this 7-hour period in the female reproductive tract?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because capacitation involves the removal of a glycoprotein coat and seminal plasma proteins from the plasma membrane overlying the acrosomal region. Option A is incorrect because the sperm nucleus is already condensed; it actually swells later inside the oocyte. Option B is incorrect as the tail length does not change, though motility patterns may. Option D is incorrect because the release of enzymes occurs during the acrosome reaction, which happens after capacitation. Option E is incorrect because sperm are haploid gametes and remain so until fertilization.

options.A: The sperm nucleus undergoes rapid condensation.
options.B: The tail increases in length to improve motility.
options.C: A glycoprotein coat is removed from the plasma membrane over the acrosome.
options.D: The acrosome releases trypsin-like enzymes into the uterine lumen.
options.E: The sperm restores its diploid number of chromosomes.

questions.question: During Phase 3 of fertilization, the initial adhesion between the spermatozoon and the oocyte is mediated by specific molecular interactions. Which pair of molecules is primarily responsible for this adhesion?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the initial adhesion between the sperm and egg membranes is mediated by the interaction of integrins located on the oocyte surface and their ligands, disintegrins, on the sperm. Option A is incorrect because ZP3 and acrosin are involved in Phase 2 (penetration of the zona pellucida). Option C is incorrect as selectins are primarily involved in the attachment of the blastocyst to the uterine wall during implantation. Option D is incorrect because these are steroid hormones, not primary adhesion molecules for gamete fusion. Option E is incorrect as hyaluronidase helps sperm pass through the corona radiata, not the final membrane adhesion.

options.A: ZP3 on the zona and acrosin on the sperm
options.B: Integrins on the oocyte and disintegrins on the sperm
options.C: L-selectin on the sperm and carbohydrate receptors on the oocyte
options.D: Progesterone on the oocyte and testosterone on the sperm
options.E: Hyaluronidase on the sperm and collagen on the oocyte

questions.question: One of the definitive results of fertilization is the introduction of genetic variation in the offspring. How is this variation primarily achieved during the fertilization process?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the zygote contains a new combination of chromosomes derived from two different parents, which is distinct from both the mother and the father. Option A is incorrect because the completion of meiosis II only ensures the oocyte is haploid. Option B is incorrect because while restoration of diploidy is a result, it describes the quantity of chromosomes, not the qualitative variation. Option D is incorrect as metabolic activation refers to the initiation of embryogenesis. Option E is incorrect because the second polar body is a discarded byproduct of meiosis.

options.A: Through the completion of the second meiotic division
options.B: By the restoration of the diploid number of chromosomes
options.C: Through the intermingling of maternal and paternal chromosomes
options.D: By the metabolic activation of the oocyte
options.E: Through the formation of the second polar body

questions.question: A couple is struggling with infertility, and the male partner is found to have a very low sperm count (oligozoospermic). They are advised to undergo Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection (ICSI). In this procedure, the "metabolic activation of the egg" must still occur for successful development. What normally carries the activating factor into the oocyte during natural fertilization?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the activating factor that triggers the initial cellular and molecular events of embryogenesis (metabolic activation) is carried by the spermatozoon into the oocyte. Option A is incorrect as follicular fluid is left behind in the ovary or enters the tube but does not activate the egg. Option B is incorrect because corona radiata cells are external barriers. Option D is incorrect because zona proteins facilitate binding and the acrosome reaction, not metabolic activation. Option E is incorrect because while the tube provides the environment for capacitation, it does not provide the activation trigger.

questions.question: Following the successful entry of the sperm into the oocyte, the male and female pronuclei approach each other. Which of the following describes the correct sequence of events leading to the two-cell stage?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the pronuclei first replicate their DNA while still separate, then their envelopes disappear, and the maternal and paternal chromosomes (each consisting of two sister chromatids) organize on a common mitotic spindle before the cell divides. Option A is incorrect because the pronuclear membranes do not fuse; they break down. Option C is incorrect because DNA replication must occur before the chromosomes can intermingle on the spindle. Option D is incorrect because there is no "fusion of haploid nuclei" into a single nucleus before the first division. Option E is incorrect because meiosis II must be completed before the female pronucleus can even form.

options.A: Fusion of pronuclear membranes -> DNA replication -> Spindle formation -> Cleavage
options.B: DNA replication -> Loss of pronuclear envelopes -> Chromosome intermingling on spindle -> Cleavage
options.C: Chromosome intermingling -> DNA replication -> Nuclear fusion -> Cleavage
options.D: Loss of envelopes -> Fusion of haploid nuclei -> DNA replication -> Cleavage
options.E: DNA replication -> Fusion of pronuclear membranes -> Meiosis II completion -> Cleavage

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: Which of the following medical procedures is specifically defined as the clinical handling of both human oocytes and sperm, or embryos, for the purpose of establishing a pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because Assisted Reproductive Technology (ART) is the broad term encompassing medical procedures like IVF and ICSI where both the egg and sperm are handled outside the body. Option A is incorrect because artificial insemination only involves the handling of sperm, not oocytes. Option B is incorrect because ovulation induction uses drugs to stimulate the ovaries but does not involve the extracorporeal handling of gametes. Option C is incorrect because hormone replacement therapy is used to manage hormonal deficiencies and is not a technique for handling gametes. Option E is incorrect because while it is a type of ART, it is a specific sub-technique, whereas the question asks for the general definition of the category.

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: A 29-year-old woman with a history of recurrent pelvic inflammatory disease is found to have extensive scarring that has blocked both of her fallopian tubes. This anatomical cause of infertility primarily prevents which of the following?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the fallopian tubes are the site of fertilization; anatomical blockage prevents the sperm from reaching the oocyte in the ampulla. Option A is incorrect because tubal scarring does not typically stop the ovaries from producing oocytes. Option C is incorrect because implantation occurs in the uterus, not the tubes, although tubal damage prevents the embryo from reaching the uterus. Option D is incorrect because theca cells are located in the ovarian follicles, and their function is independent of tubal patency. Option E is incorrect because the regression of the corpus albicans is a hormonal process in the ovary and is not affected by the state of the fallopian tubes.

options.B: Physical meeting of sperm and oocyte
options.D: Secretion of estrogen by theca cells

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: A 30-year-old male is being evaluated for primary infertility. His medical history reveals that he had bilateral undescended testes (cryptorchidism) that were not surgically corrected until he was 6 years old. A semen analysis now shows a complete absence of sperm (azoospermia). What is the most likely physiological basis for his infertility?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because spermatogenesis requires a temperature lower than the core body temperature; prolonged exposure of the testes to the higher temperature of the abdominal cavity in cryptorchidism leads to the degeneration of the germinal epithelium. Option A is incorrect because cryptorchidism affects sperm production in the seminiferous tubules rather than causing a mechanical blockage of the ducts. Option B is incorrect because the issue is a primary testicular failure due to temperature, not a secondary failure of the pituitary gland. Option C is incorrect because androgen receptors are usually present, but the cells that would respond to them (spermatogonia) have been damaged. Option D is incorrect because capacitation occurs in the female reproductive tract and is irrelevant if no sperm are produced.

options.B: Failure of the pituitary to secrete LH
options.D: Inability of sperm to undergo capacitation
options.E: Damage to the germinal epithelium from abdominal temperature

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: In a standard In Vitro Fertilization (IVF) protocol, oocytes are recovered from the ovarian follicles using an aspirator under ultrasound guidance. At what specific stage of maturation are these oocytes typically retrieved to ensure the highest success rate?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because oocytes are recovered just before ovulation when they are in the late stages of the first meiotic division, ensuring they are mature enough to be fertilized. Option A is incorrect because primary follicles contain immature oocytes that are not yet ready for fertilization. Option B is incorrect because meiosis II is only completed after a sperm has successfully penetrated the oocyte. Option D is incorrect because the corpus luteum forms only after the oocyte has already been discharged from the follicle. Option E is incorrect because the antrum is a feature of vesicular follicles, and oocytes must reach the mature vesicular (Graafian) stage before retrieval.

options.A: During the early primary follicle stage
options.B: Just after the completion of meiosis II
options.C: During the late stages of the first meiotic division
options.D: Immediately after the formation of the corpus luteum
options.E: Before the appearance of the antrum

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: A 31-year-old woman undergoing treatment for irregular ovulation is prescribed clomiphene citrate. During counseling, the physician explains that this medication carries a significantly higher risk of multiple births compared to natural conception. What is the physiological mechanism responsible for this outcome?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because clomiphene citrate stimulates the release of higher concentrations of Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH), which can rescue multiple follicles from atresia, leading to multiple ovulations. Option B is incorrect because the maintenance of the corpus luteum is handled by hCG after implantation, not by clomiphene. Option C is incorrect because the oropharyngeal membrane is an embryonic structure and its rupture is not related to ovulation or clomiphene. Option D is incorrect because clomiphene increases the number of eggs ovulated (dizygotic twinning) rather than causing a single embryo to split (monozygotic twinning). Option E is incorrect because fimbrial movement is a mechanical process and not the primary reason for multiple gestations following drug therapy.

options.A: It increases FSH levels, stimulating the maturation of multiple follicles
options.B: It prevents the degeneration of the corpus luteum of pregnancy
options.C: It induces the premature rupture of the oropharyngeal membrane
options.D: It causes the splitting of the inner cell mass at the blastocyst stage
options.E: It enhances the contractility of the fimbriae to collect multiple eggs

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: Hostile cervical mucus is a recognized physiological cause of female infertility. Under normal conditions, which hormonal change is required to thin the cervical mucus and allow the passage of sperm into the uterus?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the production of estrogens by the granulosa and theca cells during the follicular phase causes the thinning of cervical mucus, making it permeable to sperm. Option A is incorrect because a drop in progesterone leads to menstruation, which is not the time for sperm passage. Option C is incorrect because oocyte maturation inhibitor (OMI) is a peptide that keeps the oocyte arrested in prophase and does not affect cervical mucus. Option D is incorrect because relaxin is involved in softening the cervix and ligaments during labor, not during the mid-cycle for sperm transport. Option E is incorrect because human somatomammotropin is a placental hormone involved in fetal growth and maternal glucose regulation.

options.A: A sudden drop in progesterone levels
options.C: The secretion of oocyte maturation inhibitor
options.D: The release of relaxin from the pelvic ligaments

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: A male patient's semen analysis reveals a normal sperm count and morphology, but the sperm exhibit very low motility (asthenozoospermia). In which part of the male reproductive tract do spermatozoa normally acquire their full motility?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because while sperm are produced in the testes, they are initially non-motile and only acquire full motility during their passage through the epididymis. Option A is incorrect because sperm in the seminiferous tubules are immature and lack the ability to swim. Option B is incorrect because the rete testis is a collection point within the testis where sperm are still non-motile. Option C is incorrect because the efferent ductules transport sperm to the epididymis but are not the primary site of motility acquisition. Option D is incorrect because the prostatic urethra is part of the exit path during ejaculation, long after motility should have been acquired.

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: Following the addition of sperm to retrieved oocytes in an IVF laboratory, the embryologist must confirm that fertilization has occurred before the embryos can be transferred. Which of the following observations is the definitive sign that fertilization has successfully taken place?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the presence of two pronuclei (one male and one female) within the oocyte cytoplasm is the standard morphological evidence that the sperm has entered and the oocyte has completed its second meiotic division. Option A is incorrect because the zona pellucida only disappears (hatches) at the blastocyst stage, several days after fertilization. Option B is incorrect because the syncytiotrophoblast is a derivative of the trophoblast that forms during the second week of development. Option C is incorrect because a fertilized egg should have two polar bodies (the second is formed upon completion of meiosis II at fertilization). Option E is incorrect because the decidua reaction occurs in the maternal uterine stroma after implantation.

options.A: The disappearance of the zona pellucida
options.C: The presence of a single polar body
options.E: The initiation of the decidua reaction

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: A 34-year-old woman has been unable to conceive for three years. A pelvic ultrasound reveals several large intramural leiomyomas (uterine fibroids) that significantly distort the shape of the uterine cavity. What is the most likely anatomical basis for her infertility?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because distortion of the uterine cavity by fibroids can prevent the blastocyst from properly attaching to and embedding within the endometrium. Option B is incorrect because fibroids are uterine tumors and do not typically prevent the ovaries from releasing oocytes. Option C is incorrect because the acrosome reaction occurs in the fallopian tube upon contact with the zona pellucida, not in the uterine cavity. Option D is incorrect because the pericardioperitoneal canals are embryonic structures related to the development of the body cavities, not adult fertility. Option E is incorrect because the primitive streak forms within the embryo itself after implantation has already occurred.

options.E: Failure of the primitive streak to form

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: A couple is discussing their options for fertility treatment and decides to try Intrauterine Insemination (IUI) before considering In Vitro Fertilization (IVF). What is the fundamental procedural difference between these two techniques?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because IUI involves placing washed sperm directly into the uterus so fertilization can occur naturally in the fallopian tubes, while IVF involves combining eggs and sperm in a culture dish outside the body. Option A is incorrect because both IUI and IVF can use either the couple's own gametes or donor gametes. Option B is incorrect because the injection of a single sperm into an egg is called Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection (ICSI), which is a variation of IVF. Option D is incorrect because both techniques can be used for various causes of infertility. Option E is incorrect because IUI still requires at least one functional fallopian tube for the sperm to meet the egg.

options.A: IUI requires the use of donor oocytes, while IVF does not.
options.B: IUI involves the injection of a single sperm into the egg.
options.C: IUI results in fertilization inside the mother's body, whereas IVF occurs in a lab.
options.D: IUI is only used for female factor infertility, while IVF is for male factor.
options.E: IUI bypasses the need for the fallopian tubes entirely.

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: A woman is diagnosed with a "luteal phase defect" during an infertility workup. This physiological condition is primarily characterized by the inadequate production of which hormone, which is essential for preparing the endometrium for implantation?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because progesterone, produced by the corpus luteum, is responsible for the secretory phase of the endometrium; inadequate levels prevent the uterus from becoming receptive to the blastocyst. Option A is incorrect because while LH triggers ovulation and forms the corpus luteum, the "defect" refers to the subsequent output of the corpus luteum itself. Option C is incorrect because estriol is a major estrogen of pregnancy produced by the placenta, not the primary hormone of the luteal phase. Option D is incorrect because hCG is produced by the embryo after implantation to maintain the corpus luteum. Option E is incorrect because testosterone is a male androgen and is not the primary hormone responsible for endometrial preparation.

questions.lo: F-A-019. Infertility and assisted reproductive techniques
questions.question: During the clinical management of an IVF cycle, the embryologist may need to manually remove a specific layer of cells surrounding the oocyte to perform Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection (ICSI). What is the name of this outermost cellular layer?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the corona radiata is the layer of granulosa cells that remains attached to the oocyte after ovulation; it must be removed for the embryologist to visualize the oocyte clearly for ICSI. Option A is incorrect because the zona pellucida is the glycoprotein shell immediately surrounding the oocyte, which is not removed but rather pierced during ICSI. Option B is incorrect because the exocoelomic (Heuser) membrane is an embryonic structure that forms the primitive yolk sac. Option C is incorrect because the theca interna is a layer of the ovarian follicle that remains in the ovary to become part of the corpus luteum. Option D is incorrect because the hypoblast is a layer of the bilaminar germ disc formed after implantation.

questions.question: Which specific cell population gives rise to the amnioblasts that line the amniotic cavity?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because amnioblasts are specifically derived from epiblast cells that are adjacent to the cytotrophoblast; together with the rest of the epiblast, they form the lining of the amniotic cavity. Option A is incorrect because the cytotrophoblast is the inner layer of the trophoblast and does not contribute to the amniotic lining. Option C is incorrect because the hypoblast forms the ventral layer of the bilaminar disc and contributes to the yolk sac lining. Option D is incorrect because the syncytiotrophoblast is the outer, multinucleated layer of the trophoblast involved in erosion of maternal tissues. Option E is incorrect because the extraembryonic mesoderm forms later between the trophoblast and the primitive yolk sac.

questions.question: On the ninth day of development, what specific structure temporarily seals the penetration defect in the surface epithelium of the endometrium?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the fibrin coagulum is the specific plug that closes the original defect in the uterine surface epithelium as the blastocyst becomes more deeply embedded. Option A is incorrect because syncytial knots are pieces of syncytium that break off into the maternal circulation later in pregnancy.
